text: Hello everyone, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California. I present to you a case of a robotic tap inguinal hernia repair where there is no indirect hernia sac.
The surgeon examines the left side of the groin, where a structure appears to herniate through the abdominal wall, suggesting the presence of an indirect inguinal hernia.
The surgeon performs a flap dissection on the left side of the groin for a recurrent inguinal hernia, following previous dissection on the contralateral side.

The surgeon navigates through the surgical planes, having opened the right side, revealing fatty tissue in the space of Retzius. The dissection leaves a significant amount of fat anteriorly, prompting the surgeon to check for any fat herniating through a defect, which is not observed.

The surgeon approaches the indirect space in a recurrent inguinal hernia repair. The foreground shows the sigmoid colon, confirming that the herniated structure is retroperitoneal fat rather than colon.

The surgeon navigates the plane between the testicular vessels and retroperitoneal fat, identifying the white structure as the vas deferens rather than transverse fascia. A layer of tissue is separated from the surrounding fatty tissue, revealing the herniating structures.

The surgeon explores the retroperitoneal fat, encountering a larger vessel that begins to bleed upon division, indicating a misstep into the wrong plane. Efforts are made to reduce the fat and separate the vas deferens, which is surrounded by an unusual scar. The surgeon works laterally to locate the testicular vessels and removes fat from the peritoneum, which is visible and easily accessible.

The surgeon identifies a distinct color difference in the fat, with orange testicular vessel fat on the left and lighter yellow colonic retroperitoneal fat on the right. A plane is found to separate the orange fat, which contains larger vessels consistent with testicular vessels, from the lighter fat.

The surgeon observes the testicular vessels positioned more laterally than usual, with retroperitoneal fat situated between the vessels and the vas deferens. The orangey fat is being pulled onto the retroperitoneal fat, and the surgeon aims to remove the lighter fat from the inguinal canal while leaving the orangey fat intact.

The surgeon retracts the testicular vessel fat from the retroperitoneal fat, using the left hand for effective retraction. Hemostasis is maintained to differentiate between the testicular fatty tissue and the retroperitoneal fatty tissue. The surgeon continues to reduce the retroperitoneal fat and identifies a large window between the vas deferens and the retroperitoneal fat, indicating the final section of retroperitoneal fat to be divided.

The surgeon identifies a vessel within the retroperitoneal fat and cauterizes it to prevent retraction and potential bleeding. The retroperitoneal fat is mostly separated, revealing a plane between the fat and structures on the psoas muscle. The surgeon carefully works to find the plane between the vas deferens and the retroperitoneal fat, ensuring not to injure the nerves located posteriorly. A clear plane is visible as the retroperitoneal fatty tissue is manipulated.
The surgeon identifies the herniated tissue protruding through the indirect space and proceeds to create additional space for the placement of the surgical mesh.
The surgeon places an extra-large 3D Max mesh in the mid-abdomen, ensuring there is sufficient space for the mesh to overlap, particularly posteriorly, to secure it effectively.

The surgeon navigates through a scarred area, revealing loose connective tissue with the vas deferens positioned distantly. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, femoral branch, and genital branch of the genital femoral nerve are identified, with attempts to preserve fatty tissue on the nerves to prevent mesh exposure.
The surgeon advances two centimeters deep into the space of Retzius, following the completion of the main dissection.
The surgeon ensures that the bladder and retroperitoneal tissue are retracted away from the vas deferens to allow the mesh to transition upwards. The lateral dissection appears satisfactory.

The surgeon identifies the testicular vessels laterally along the abdominal wall while carefully dissecting around the obturator nerve, artery, and vein, as well as the external iliac vein. The patient is in a steep Trendelenburg position, and the surgeon proceeds cautiously to tuck the mesh around the medial and inguinal ligament.

The surgeon tucks the mesh vertically into the space of Retzius, ensuring the bladder is positioned away from the pubis. The surgeon examines the abdominal wall for defects by pressing on it, assessing for a direct inguinal hernia. The fat is manipulated to check for herniation through a defect, but visually, it does not appear to be present.
The surgeon leaves most of the fat on the ceiling of the surgical field, ensuring that the space is adequate to accommodate two pieces of mesh.

The surgeon positions the left side of the 3D Max mid extra-large mesh, aligning it vertically over the vas deferens and along the inguinal ligament. The mesh is tucked into the space of Retzius, ensuring it remains stable when the bladder distends. A small vessel on the bladder is observed bleeding. The mesh is sutured to Cooper's ligament with a 3-0 Vicryl suture and secured superior laterally to prevent counterclockwise lifting.

So you can imagine if I came in in a TEP fashion, looked in there, all that fat would have looked like just normal retroperitoneal fat and the hernia could be missed. This is probably a recurrence that i see from other surgeons probably once every two or three years so it's not a frequent occurrence but just be aware of that thank you very much
